title: Observation of Erratic Non-Hermitian Skin Effect in Phononic Crystals
authors: Yujian Yuan, Jie Liu, He Gao, Jiamin Guo, Zhongming Gu, Jie Zhu
abstract: The erratic non-Hermitian skin effect (ENHSE), emerging from the interplay between disorders and locally nonreciprocal yet globally reciprocal couplings, has reshaped the conventional bulk-boundary correspondence through its disorder-dependent localization properties. Here, we experimentally observe the dynamical phenomena of ENHSE in phononic crystals with disordered imaginary gauge fields. The erratic localization occurs in the bulk independent of the excitation position, with the main and satellite peaks precisely located at the local maxima of the cumulative gauge field in accordance with random-walk extreme-value statistics. Remarkably, the selective manipulation of satellite peaks can be realized by tuning the staggered disorder strengths in a dimerized chain. These findings can deepen the understanding of non-Hermitian physics and establish a new route for disorder-engineered non-Hermitian wave control.
summary: This paper reports the experimental observation of the Erratic Non-Hermitian Skin Effect in a phononic crystal. Unlike the standard non-Hermitian skin effect which pushes energy to the edges, this effect creates localized peaks within the bulk of the system. These peaks are determined by the extreme values of a random-walk-like gauge field created by disordered nonreciprocal couplings. The authors also show that these peaks can be deterministically manipulated by tuning the disorder in a dimerized lattice structure.
main_problem: The study investigates the Erratic Non-Hermitian Skin Effect (ENHSE), where disorder and local nonreciprocity cause wave functions to form discrete bulk peaks rather than accumulating at boundaries.
main_result: The researchers experimentally observed that bulk localization peaks in a phononic crystal correspond to the local maxima of a cumulative gauge field, following random-walk extreme-value statistics, and demonstrated the ability to steer these peaks via sublattice tuning.
method: The work combines experimental measurements of acoustic pressure in a 1D phononic crystal with large-scale numerical simulations using the Crank-Nicolson method and random-walk statistical analysis.
model_or_system: A 1D phononic crystal consisting of resonant cavities and tubes, implementing a disordered Hatano-Nelson model and a dimerized Su-Schellig-Heeger (SSH) model with nonreciprocal couplings via an electronic amplifier circuit.
key_observables: Spatial eigenstate distribution, acoustic pressure profiles, real-space winding number, and cumulative gauge field trajectories.
important_parameters: Disorder strength (delta h), mean bias (h-bar), lattice size (N), and the probability of positive/negative coupling (b1, b2).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the weak-bias regime where the average non-reciprocity vanishes (h-bar = 0).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Hatano-Nelson model and the correspondence between random-walk distributions and eigenstate peaks; Figure 4 illustrates the nonreciprocal SSH model, winding numbers, and the spatial alignment of acoustic pressure with random-walk trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ENHSE concept, describes the experimental phononic platform and nonreciprocal mechanism, presents numerical validation of the localization mechanism via extreme-value statistics, and concludes with the demonstration of sublattice-specific wave control.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates a new regime of non-Hermitian localization driven by stochastic fluctuations rather than global topology, offering a method for disorder-engineered wave control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Erratic Non-Hermitian Skin Effect. (a) Schematic diagram of the one-dimensional non-reciprocal Hatano–Nelson model with N sites. (b) Comparison of random-walk distributions and eigenstate distributions under open boundary conditions (OBC) for N = 1999. (c) Eigenenergy spectra under periodic boundary conditions (PBC) and OBC. (d) Winding numbers averaged over 50 randomly generated sequences for different mean values, with lattice size N = 1999. (e) Time evolution of a random walk process. (f) Random-walk distributions and eigenstate distributions under OBC for N = 20. The shaded light-blue area represents the wave packet of the corresponding eigenstates. (g) Numerically simulated...

FIG. 2. Experimental Implementation. (a) Equivalent Hatano–Nelson model with the same mathematical structure. (b) Top-view photograph of a one-dimensional disordered acoustic crystal. Non-reciprocal coupling is implemented via an amplifier (with DC power supply), a microphone (input), and a speaker (output). Source and detector are used to mea- sure transmission. The green and yellow arrows denote the reciprocal coupling and the unidirectional nonreciprocal cou- pling, respectively. (c) Photograph of a one-dimensional dis- ordered acoustic crystal with 20 resonators.

Fig. 2(a). By randomly assigning the sign of hn according to a Bernoulli distribution, we determine the direction of the non-reciprocal coupling between neighboring sites, thereby directly introducing disorder into the lattice. The one-dimensional acoustic lattice consists of res- onant cavities that serve as discrete lattice sites. Ad- jacent sites are coupled through connecting tubes that provide the reciprocal coupling κ0. Figure 2(b) pro- vides a close-up view of the experimental implementa- tion, highlighting the site-resolved cavity arrangement and inter-site coupling pathways. Each cavity contains a microphone–speaker pair linked through an external amplifier circuit to realize the...

FIG. 3. Experimental results of the one-dimensional disordered Hatano–Nelson model. (a) Acoustic pressure distributions for excitation at site 8 (1721 Hz), site 12 (1715 Hz), and site 15 (1719 Hz) under a fixed disordered configuration, the upper curves correspond to the random-walk trajectories. (b) Acoustic pressure distributions at three frequencies for excitation at site 12. (c–d) Same as panels (a–b), with excitation applied at site 3 (1728 Hz), site 7 (1715 Hz), and site 12 (1736 Hz). The acoustic pressure distributions at three representative frequencies are shown.

FIG. 5. Selective manipulation of localization peaks. His- tograms of the logarithmic peak amplitudes ln |ψpeak| for the most localized eigenstates. Blue and purple bars indicate spatial peaks pinned to sublattice A and sublattice B, re- spectively. The parameters are set to (a) ∆h1 = 0.5, ∆h2 = 1.0, b1 = 0.9, b2 = 0.3, and (b) ∆h1 = 1.0, ∆h2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.3, b2 = 0.9.

FIG. 4. Experimental results of the one-dimensional dis- ordered SSH model. (a) Schematic diagram of the one- dimensional non-reciprocal SSH model with N sites. (b) Winding numbers averaged over 50 randomly generated se- quences for different mean values, with lattice size N = 1999. (c) Acoustic pressure distributions for excitation at site 5 (1724 Hz), site 8 (1722 Hz), and site 15 (1719 Hz) under a fixed disordered configuration, the upper curves correspond to the random-walk trajectories. (d) Acoustic pressure distri- butions at three frequencies for excitation at site 12.
